findings.message: N+1 query pattern: `get_item_names` first fetches all item IDs in one query (line 45), then issues a separate `session.get(Item, iid)` query for every row in the loop. On a table with N rows this executes N+1 database round-trips. The endpoint has no pagination limit, so the number of queries is unbounded and scales linearly with table size.
findings.suggestion: Replace the two-step ID fetch + per-row load with a single query that projects only the `name` column: `return list(session.scalars(select(Item.name)))`. This reduces the total query count from N+1 to 1 and avoids loading full ORM objects when only one field is needed. If the result set must be bounded, apply `.limit(MAX_PAGE_SIZE)` consistent with the existing `list_items` repository pattern.

findings.message: Unbounded read with no pagination: `select(Item.id)` fetches every ID in the table with no LIMIT or OFFSET. Combined with the N+1 loop below, this means the endpoint will issue one query per row in the entire `items` table, making it a denial-of-service vector on large tables.
findings.suggestion: Apply a hard cap (e.g. `.limit(MAX_PAGE_SIZE)`) on the initial ID query, or — preferably — rewrite as a single `select(Item.name).limit(MAX_PAGE_SIZE)` query consistent with how `list_items` in the repository bounds its reads.
